words: HELEN KELLBERG ANDERSON: Dad was always handing out money to 'em, money to the boys.
I thought the girls had it a little harder to get money to spend and so forth.
Boys had it easier coming you know. He'd say, "Oh, do you need some money?"
But you know, I always felt that the girls had it harder that way. But, oh dad,
you know he was pretty well-to-do, but not flush or anything like that, but
always had to struggle to make money every year. But they never mentioned about
me going on to school. I don't know why. And I guess I was one of those people,
I just didn't speak up enough, 'cause I find myself today sometimes in a pre
dicament (laughs) 'cause Idon't stand up for myself sometimes, so that's the way
it is. So I guess it's my timidness. Then I'd probably feel bad about it, and
then keep it to myself. (Laughs)
LAURA SCHRAGER: I'd like to ask you some things about when you were a kid, that you
were telling me before.

speaker: HA
words: Oh yeah, yeah, I was always doing something. I was the one that'd always have

speaker: HA
words: to do things, oh well, I wanted to. That was the one thing, I had to be doing

speaker: HA
words: something. But I was the one that noticed everything, I saw everything, and of

speaker: HA
words: course that's the one reason I probably would get in dutch because I saw more.

speaker: HA
words: People wouldn't have to say anything, nobody would have to mention it, but if

speaker: HA
words: it was anything why I'd see it, and I'd know it, and I couldn't help it, it was

speaker: HA
words: just my nature.

speaker: HA
words: But anyway, we always went after the cows, and we went—oh the canyon was

speaker: HA
words: 'bout three-fourths of a mile deep. But we'd have to go over about three different

speaker: HA
words: hills, you know go down and go further south, and every night we'd bring the cows

speaker: HA
words: home. And oh, we'd always have six, seven, maybe eight milk cows and some extra

speaker: HA
words: calves or something. And drive 'em up to the top of the hill, and then of course

speaker: HA
words: we'd exercise hunting for those cows, and then coming out of the canyon the cows

speaker: HA
words: would always beat us you know. Walk so much faster, or else they'd poke and we'd

speaker: HA
words: have to go back and get them. So anyway, this is every night we did that. And

speaker: HA
words: it was either Ted and I, or Layton and I, somebody practically always was with

speaker: HA
words: me. But, one night I wasn't with them, I guess I was doing something else, in

speaker: HA
words: later years when Layton and Teddy I think was supposed to get the cows. And they

speaker: HA
words: hunted for three days, and they'd come home and wouldn't find the cows. And dad

speaker: HA
words: said, "We've got to milk those cows." They won't milk you know, if you go too

speaker: HA
words: long you ruin the cow. And so they went for about three nights trying to find

speaker: HA
words: 'em and they never come. And dad thought they were just maybe scared of going

speaker: HA
words: down 'cause of bears or something you know. But it wasn't that. So then dad,

speaker: HA
words: he got on his horse and he went down there. And there was no herd laws in those

speaker: HA
words: days and not very many fences, and so they went from this east pasture clear

speaker: HA
words: around Trout's pasture and up the canyon. Followed the railroad track and came

speaker: HA
words: out at Troy. (Laughs) So he found the cows just as you come into Troy there.

speaker: HA
words: So you can see why the boys couldn't find the cows.

speaker: HA
words: And, oh, we used to go down, there was so many houses in the canyon at that

speaker: HA
words: time. There was the Cusic house. I don't know too much about 'em, but the older

speaker: HA
words: children knew more about 'em than I did, but they moved away. Then there was a

speaker: HA
words: Wheeler house and it was a log cabin. And this one, it was quite a large long

speaker: HA
words: room and it was all papered with German newspapers and so we couldn't read »em.

speaker: HA
words: But it was that, what you called this handwriting, you know, so curled up that

speaker: HA
words: you can't hardly understand it, you know...

speaker: LS
words: Oh, it was the old fashioned German script...

speaker: HA
words: Yeah script. I think it's beautiful, but I couldn't read anything. 'Course

speaker: HA
words: I probably wouldn't have been that good in German anyway. But anyway, it was

speaker: HA
words: that kind of words so you couldn't begin to try and read. Then...

speaker: LS
words: Did they have little gardens?

speaker: HA
words: Yes they had...I didn't know too much about the gardens, because they too had

speaker: HA
words: pulled out. I guess they couldn't just live and make a living there. Although

speaker: HA
words: I think some of that soil was nice and rich down there, but there wasn't enough

speaker: HA
words: of it. But they had planted bluegrass lawns, and they were well-kept, and a

speaker: HA
words: fence around it. And there was apple trees and fruit trees and berry bushes,

speaker: HA
words: like gooseberries and currants and blackberries. And then they always had such

speaker: HA
words: pretty flowers, they had sweet williams and other flowers that we'd go down

speaker: HA
words: there and pick many years afterwards. And they would grow up in the brush around

speaker: HA
words: way up on the lawn. Oh, they'd grow almost two and a half, three feet tall. And

speaker: HA
words: then they'd have these great big heads almost as big as saucers, they were so

speaker: HA
words: pretty.

speaker: HA
words: We finally quit going down there because my cousins moved away and we were

speaker: HA
words: alone, but we used to go once or twice a year down there. Oh they had some apples

speaker: HA
words: too, tasted always good, stray apples always tasted good to us. But then as we

speaker: HA
words: got older and so forth we didn't go down there. But one thing I think scared us,

speaker: HA
words: that's when we went with our cousins, two cousins, Gladys and Alice Oiler. And

speaker: HA
words: we took a notion to follow the fence line, we put a new fence in there, so we

speaker: HA
words: followed the fence line clear to the bottom of the creek there. And thought we

speaker: HA
words: was going to play around there, but we saw a bunch of tiny snakes, they were

speaker: HA
words: almost white. I guess they was newly born and they had such big heads on 'em.

speaker: HA
words: So we figured they were rattlers, so we decided we'd better get out of there while

speaker: HA
words: the gettin* was good. And so we walked back, and so I think that's about the

speaker: HA
words: last time we've been down in that canyon.

speaker: HA
words: But there was other...the Porter place was interesting. We used to go down

speaker: HA
words: there after they left. They used to be...went to Burnt Ridge school. And the

speaker: HA
words: girls' names were Mammie and Isabel. And the boy's name was Sam. And they could

speaker: HA
words: have had another sister, but I don't remember. But anyway, we used to walk to

speaker: HA
words: school, and the older kids that were higher up in grade school and we always had

speaker: HA
words: a good time, walking to school. And there was the Pearson girls, I think they

speaker: HA
words: were four girls in the Pearson family. And there was Selma Ruberg and Edith

speaker: HA
words: Carlson and Minnie Oiler, and then Emma and Gladys and Alice, so there was quite

speaker: HA
words: a few young girls going to school. 'Course they always seemed like they were so

speaker: HA
words: grown-up that you'd think that they were almost twenty years old. They weren't

speaker: HA
words: that old, but they wore longer dresses and everything, and ah, they were so tall

speaker: HA
words: and I was just a little tyke. But anyway...

speaker: LS
words: Do you remember the canyon getting logged at all?

speaker: HA
words: Oh yeah, yes I do. I want to say something more about the time when I was a

speaker: HA
words: little girl and went to school. But in those days everybody had to have a gate

speaker: HA
words: to go into the yard with, big big board gates you know, with several boards across

speaker: HA
words: and supports and hooks that fasten your gate to the fence to open and shut.

speaker: HA
words: Everybody had a gate. I think it's because they used to pasture—well it was the

speaker: HA
words: style, something new, so everybody had a gate. So all these women especially

speaker: HA
words: Selma Ruberg and the Oiler girls and the Pearson girls and I don't know, Edith

speaker: HA
words: was older and practically out of school. Anyway, they'd all congregate there

speaker: HA
words: you know, and talk about the books like Annie of Green Gables, and all the Pepper's

speaker: HA
words: family and Tom Sawyer, and what's this other one?

speaker: LS
words: You'd get together and talk about it?

speaker: HA
words: Well they'd talk about them, and Little Women and so forth. And they used to

speaker: HA
words: talk about one—that Helen and her babies, but I don't know which book that was

speaker: HA
words: in now. And anyway, they'd mention that, and then they'd look at me because I

speaker: HA
words: was Helen you know. And so I heard exactly what they said and kind of laughed

speaker: HA
words: when I went by. Talking about Helen and her babies, and I didn't know whether

speaker: HA
words: that was in Little Women or a Pepper book or (Uncle Tom's Cabin too was an

speaker: HA
words: interesting book. The teacher used to read these books five minutes after noon

speaker: HA
words: recess. Kind of to settle the kids down. So they were interesting books.)

speaker: HA
words: But anyway, they made the remark of Helen and her babies you know, and I'd walk

speaker: HA
words: past you know and not pay any attention to it. So then this year, or was it

speaker: HA
words: last year. I wrote to Minnie and I said, "I remember how you used to talk about

speaker: HA
words: Helen and her babies. But I wouldn't let on that I heard what you said. But

speaker: HA
words: I just walked by." And so she wrote back and she said, "I didn't think you

speaker: HA
words: remembered that." (Laughs) But if you hear any of those funny remarks you're

speaker: HA
words: bound to, kind of make you feel a little bit timid.

speaker: HA
words: Anyway, Mike Rodeen, they lived at the brick house and he couldn't start

speaker: HA
words: school, and he'd always stand out at the gate and watch us go by. And oh, he

speaker: HA
words: just loved people and so he'd holler, "Hehen. Hehen." He couldn't say the "1"

speaker: HA
words: in it. I was embarrassed to tears you know because it was like calling me hen,

speaker: HA
words: of course he meant to say Helen, but he couldn't say Helen. So I guess I didn't

speaker: HA
words: show quite so much interest you know because I liked him and all that. But you

speaker: HA
words: know he didn't know any better, he couldn't say the "1" you know. But it made

speaker: HA
words: me feel kind of let down you know cause he called me Hen in place of Helen.

speaker: HA
words: (Laughs) Oh, he was cute though. He used to have his eyes through the bar of

speaker: HA
words: the gate. And—you was asking what?

speaker: LS
words: I asked whether you remembered the logging.

speaker: HA
words: Oh yeah. And they logged all these canyons, all the trees were gone. The

speaker: HA
words: hillsides were just stripped, well you could see where they pulled the trees

speaker: HA
words: down it. They had an engine in the bottom of the canyon, they called it a

speaker: HA
words: donkey where they'd pull these trees down. They'd saw 'em and then they'd have

speaker: HA
words: the trees pulled down to the bottom. I don't know whether they chopped the limbs

speaker: HA
words: off up there or down at the bottom, but anyway then they ran 'em to the mill.

speaker: HA
words: And this creek that goes through our canyon, they had banked that and made a dam,

speaker: HA
words: and so they had a millpond down there. And oh, they shipped out an awful lot

speaker: HA
words: of timber.

speaker: HA
words: And in this millpond the salmon would come upstream to spawn, and so some

speaker: HA
words: times they'd catch a great big salmon out of there. So anyway, Lois, my daughter,

speaker: HA
words: when she was about seventh or eighth grade, she took another kid with her down

speaker: HA
words: there. They were going to fish in the creek 'cause they liked to fish. And so

speaker: HA
words: she caught a salmon, and she told this other kid, "Come and help me. I need some

speaker: HA
words: help." Because the fish were too big for a hook. But he thought she'd maybe

speaker: HA
words: got just some little old tiny fish, and he wasn't about to want to come down

speaker: HA
words: there and see that. So anyway, she finally got his attention and they hooked

speaker: HA
words: it, or got it two or three times, but they finally lost it you know, it got

speaker: HA
words: away from them. But there's quite deep pools in this creek, and now, even today,

speaker: HA
words: that you can go down there and maybe fish little fish.

speaker: HA
words: But the bears are eating them up now so. (Laughs) Bears used to live on

speaker: HA
words: them. Elmer Johnson, oh just two or three years ago went down there, and he's

speaker: HA
words: going to fish. He liked to go down there once a year or so and see if there's

speaker: HA
words: any fish. Well, he went down there, and there was a mother bear and a cub or two

speaker: HA
words: and the old father bear there. So the old bear got on his old hind legs and he

speaker: HA
words: started to walk toward him, and so he thought he'd better go home. So he went

speaker: HA
words: up that logging road quite a ways and he turned around, and here he's still

speaker: HA
words: coming walking. So, I think that was practically about the last time he went down

speaker: HA
words: there. But there was a little cub up in the tree, and of course they didn't like

speaker: HA
words: that very well. So we've had quite a few bears in later years. We didn't have

speaker: HA
words: so many before, because they logged there and disturbed a lot you know, but now

speaker: HA
words: they've moved off the mountains and coming down into the valleys I guess more.

speaker: LS
words: Can you tell me that story again where your mother and you saw the cougars?

speaker: HA
words: Oh yeah. This is before I ever started school. And oh, I had had pneumonia

speaker: HA
words: about three times after we'd come to Idaho. And so I wasn't very well, and I

speaker: HA
words: was sitting there on a chair kind of shaking and kind of weak. Anyway, one day

speaker: HA
words: mother told me to, "Come to the window-quick," she says, and there was two

speaker: HA
words: cougars. They had long tails, I don't know what kind they were, but anyway, they

speaker: HA
words: walked past our pla ce just like a team of horses. They were tall, about thirty

speaker: HA
words: inches tall, or maybe taller than that maybe. Anyway they were big and they

speaker: HA
words: were walking side by side. And we watched 'em as they went down the hill and

speaker: HA
words: went down to our pasture, and haven't seem 'em since.

speaker: HA
words: But there are cougars here. Different people have killed cougars off and

speaker: HA
words: on. A cow over at the machine shed was all scratched up—streaks on his back and

speaker: HA
words: they figured it was a cougar. I think some people went, I don't know whether

speaker: HA
words: they found »em, I thought maybe they had killed the cougar. That was on the upper

speaker: HA
words: end of the ridge that they found him.

speaker: LS
words: Can you tell me about the camp, the logging camp, that you used to go down

speaker: LS
words: to?

speaker: HA
words: Oh yeah. That was at the mill.

speaker: LS
words: Oh, that was the mill?

speaker: HA
words: Bovard. And when they were logging down there and Mrs. 3ohn Peterson and some

speaker: HA
words: body else was cooking down there. I don't remember who it was, her sister-in-law

speaker: HA
words: I think, and I used to know her name too. And cooked at the logging camp and

speaker: HA
words: they had, oh quite a few big buildings down there because they had bunks for

speaker: HA
words: the men. Then they had big dining rooms and so forth. And then when the mail

speaker: HA
words: came, the train only brought the mail to the people, the train would stop and

speaker: HA
words: deliver their mail there.

speaker: HA
words: But anyway, Mrs. Peterson wanted some butter, so mother churned butter and

speaker: HA
words: I took it down there, and I was all alone. I went down by myself. I went down

speaker: HA
words: our canyon, and walked clear to the bottom until I got to Bovard, which is quite

speaker: HA
words: a ways through because you pass the Carlson Canyon too. And as I was just gonna

speaker: HA
words: go around a corner of this bunkhouse there was a great big rock about as high

speaker: HA
words: as this table. And there was just a path wide enough for me between this rock

speaker: HA
words: and the corner of this bunkhouse path to get passed, there was a trail there.

speaker: HA
words: Well, there was a great big snake and it just darted under this big rock. Well,

speaker: HA
words: I went on past the corner, and went into the house and brought this butter. And

speaker: HA
words: then I played with their daughter Gladys Peterson quite a bit, oh for an hour or

speaker: HA
words: so...

speaker: HA
words: (End of Side A)

speaker: HA
words: ...walked past that rock and made it okay. And then I walked up the Carlson

speaker: HA
words: Canyon, and Old Joe Carlson always had some bad bulls. So I thought, "Well,

speaker: HA
words: if that bull comes after me..." I picked up a club and I said, "I'll just swing

speaker: HA
words: this around my head, and I'll be all right." But I didn't see the old bull, and

speaker: HA
words: I came up and went up to the house. And then I followed the road home, it's a

speaker: HA
words: good mile back up to our place. But anyway, it was quite a walk being alone,

speaker: HA
words: because you could see most anything that you—down there.

speaker: HA
words: And many times, when we used to go down and get the cows, we used have to

speaker: HA
words: go in that canyon too, and we'd see snakes maybe in the spring. Where the cows'd

speaker: HA
words: drink water and so forth. Big ones. But I'll always live in fear of snakes, I

speaker: HA
words: have all my life. I probably would have been a missionary if I wasn't so scared

speaker: HA
words: of snakes. (Laughs) I don't think I could have gone to a foreign country, but

speaker: HA
words: I guess maybe the Lord knew that I couldn't go with snakes although I know that

speaker: HA
words: he would protect me and so forth. But my sister went in my stead I guess. I'm

speaker: HA
words: supposed to stay home I guess, and help take care of mother and dad and so forth.

speaker: HA
words: I've been a busy person all my life, I don't know what doing, but just keeping

speaker: HA
words: the home together.

speaker: LS
words: There was a story you told me about chewing gum last time. About getting...

speaker: HA
words: Oh yeah. Oh this is good. This was when dad, well dad had worked on the road...

speaker: HA
words: did I tell you that? Dad had worked on the railroad, on the First Continental

speaker: HA
words: Railroad, where they drove the Golden Spike at Provo, Utah, where the railroad

speaker: HA
words: met over the whole United States. And dad...there was nothing for the men to

speaker: HA
words: do, so before they'd go to sleep at night they'd, they had kinda a bunkhouse or

speaker: HA
words: maybe it was some kind of a side car or something that they had. They'd go and

speaker: HA
words: gamble you know, and so dad, he gambled one night there and he lost all his money.

speaker: HA
words: And so, oh he was just beside himself because he'd lost $300 in that, and he was

speaker: HA
words: gonna leave and he didn't know what to do with himself. So he said he'd just

speaker: HA
words: have to gamble one more night. So he gambled like all get-out and got his money

speaker: HA
words: back. And that taught him a lesson that he'd never gamble anymore.

speaker: HA
words: So he got so strict with his family that they didn't even chew chewing gum,

speaker: HA
words: or play cards, or anything that was gambling—that was out. And you didn't

speaker: HA
words: smoke, you didn't chew, you didn't swear. He was really, I guess he learnt a

speaker: HA
words: lesson and that was good for us too 'cause I, my sisters and my brothers didn't

speaker: HA
words: smoke, none of em. And they didn't drink.

speaker: HA
words: And well, the chewing gum. Anyway, when the Watkins man would come, his

speaker: HA
words: name was Johns from Juliaetta, he'd always like to recognize the children so he'd

speaker: HA
words: give us gum. And of course we were so many in the family. And so, when dad

speaker: HA
words: come home from work, well here we'd be chewing this gum. And it tasted so good,

speaker: HA
words: and we'd pull it you know, or we'd pop it you know. Ohhh dad'd get so mad at us.

speaker: HA
words: Momma said, "Here comes papa, here comes papa." So we'd stick our gum maybe

speaker: HA
words: under the pantry shelf or something—you stick it to there, or under the table

speaker: HA
words: or someplace, each one had a place for their own, you know. And then of course

speaker: HA
words: we'd chew that gum you know, off and on you know for a whole week or more you

speaker: HA
words: know, whatever. You'd think about it, and go and get our gum you know and we'd

speaker: HA
words: chew it. But then finally we'd forget about it, and of course then whenever we

speaker: HA
words: wanted to clean-up why, we'd find little gum stuck here and there that we'd left

speaker: HA
words: behind. But anyway, it was kind of nice that dad was strict and he learnt his

speaker: HA
words: lesson, and it helped us too. 'Cause, he wanted us kids to be good kids, and

speaker: HA
words: they all turned out to be...mother and dad had a nice family. Never were crossing

speaker: HA
words: or real bad people you know, we all do some things to get into mischief.

speaker: HA
words: Just like...oh I tell you, many years ago, it must have been about 1910, we

speaker: HA
words: went to Ringling Brothers in Moscow, Ringling Brothers Circus. And so we saw

speaker: HA
words: all these trapeze acts and all the things that they done there, walking ropes and

speaker: HA
words: so forth. Well anyway, it was so exciting to see this circus, and I think it

speaker: HA
words: was more fascinating to the boys. Because when they got home they fixed up in

speaker: HA
words: the barn, boards attached to the rafters and the roof of the barn, and on each

speaker: HA
words: side of »em trying to stand on 'em or swing on 'em or something, you know, like

speaker: HA
words: they did in the circus. And we played around till oh, maybe somebody'd finally

speaker: HA
words: get hurt, maybe not hurt that bad but finally something stopped it. The rope

speaker: HA
words: slipped out of one end or something, somebody had a collision or something.

speaker: HA
words: But one of the crazy things, we all did it. During haying season, or after

speaker: HA
words: haying season, they always had big beams, big square beams to hold the barn

speaker: HA
words: together from one side to the other, and then beams up and down. Well we would

speaker: HA
words: dare ourself out there on these beams then we'd say, "One for the money, and two

speaker: HA
words: for the show, and three to get ready and a fourth to go!" And we went down and

speaker: HA
words: jumped on the hay. Well, that worked just fine as long as there was a lot of

speaker: HA
words: hay in the barn. There was lots of fun but we'd go out there. But it was risky

speaker: HA
words: 'cause that beam that they'd go on was so slick from putting the hay in the barn

speaker: HA
words: all the time,and it was practically shiny it was so slick. And here we'd get

speaker: HA
words: out on up there and jump down. And sometimes we'd jump, when the barn was

speaker: HA
words: almost empty—there'd be rails, fence posts and stuff in the bottom. They probably

speaker: HA
words: did that so there'd be alittle bit of air underneath so it would heat the hay,

speaker: HA
words: the hay will heat if it's a little damp. Anyway, here we was jumping without

speaker: HA
words: any hay hardly in the bottom of this. I guess we were young and didn't hurt us

speaker: HA
words: very bad.

speaker: HA
words: But I think it was...I don't know whether it could have been the 4th of

speaker: HA
words: July. But Layton, he was younger than I was, two years younger, and he was going

speaker: HA
words: to jump off. And he hit his head in this square beam and cut his forehead, a deep

speaker: HA
words: gash and dad, he sewed it. up. In those days you didn't run to the doctor, but

speaker: HA
words: he sewed up the scar on his forehead...

speaker: LS
words: Your father could just sew it up himself?

speaker: HA
words: Yeah, he sewed it and...

speaker: LS
words: Did he just get a regular needle and thread?

speaker: HA
words: They had, no...

speaker: LS
words: He must of had stuff...

speaker: HA
words: They always called it cat gut is what it was, it's made out of cat guts. That

speaker: HA
words: rots, I mean dissolves—if they use it today. So I guess it's popular, but I

speaker: HA
words: don't know what they call that kind, but that's what it was. And anyway a. simular

speaker: HA
words: thing happened to Teddy, oh he had a rough time. Well, he had several bad things

speaker: HA
words: that happened to him, but I should tell this one.

speaker: HA
words: Teddy he got erysipelas. He was playing with a kid at school, and he got

speaker: HA
words: scratched on his neck. And so mother was washing clothes when he come home in

speaker: HA
words: the afternoon, 'cause she had such a big washing with such a big family and washed

speaker: HA
words: by hand. And so she looked at it, but she thought why it was just a little

speaker: HA
words: scratch, you know shouldn't hurt, it'd go pretty soon. But she didn't think

speaker: HA
words: about putting anything on it you know, she should have put something on it,

speaker: HA
words: even turpentine would've helped. 'Course that was our old standby, was turpentine

speaker: HA
words: for every cut or hurt you got or wire cut, and we never got lockjaw or anything

speaker: HA
words: like that from it. But anyway he got erysipelas, and he was so sick, running a

speaker: HA
words: high temperature. He was there two or three weeks you know, and he just wasted

speaker: HA
words: away, and had such headache he could hardly stand it and so forth. Old Joe

speaker: HA
words: Carlson come up, our neighbor from where Carlson lived, and he said, "I'll tell

speaker: HA
words: you what you do," he said. "You grate a tub full of potatoes. And then you can

speaker: HA
words: take a big sheet and you put him in that, and wrap these potatoes all around

speaker: HA
words: him." And then I guess put a blanket around that.

speaker: LS
words: Would you cook the potatoes?

speaker: HA
words: No, you just grate 'em, raw potatoes.

speaker: LS
words: And just put 'em right on?

speaker: HA
words: And they put 'em on him. And the crisis they figured would be about two

speaker: HA
words: o'clock that night, and it'd been almost a month. And that night he went to

speaker: HA
words: sleep, but he came out of it you know, he finally come out. Well, the fever left

speaker: HA
words: him, and the headache left him, and the potatoes had drawed all this infection

speaker: HA
words: out of him, and he was just as limp and weak as a dishrag. And he lost all his

speaker: HA
words: hair, and he just had to learn to walk all over again, it was just pitiful. And

speaker: HA
words: he didn't go to school, this was in the spring and he couldn't go to school till

speaker: HA
words: the next year, and oh I don't when he did go...in the fall. He went to school

speaker: HA
words: and he had to wear a hat, he wore a blue serge cap on his head and he had a little

speaker: HA
words: blue serge suit. And he went to school in that. He had to wear that hat in the

speaker: HA
words: school too, because he'd freeze on his head you know, he didn't have any-.hair.

speaker: HA
words: But when his birthday come that following year dad bought him a nice wagon,

speaker: HA
words: coaster wagon. And I better not put this one down because somebody might read it.

speaker: HA
words: Anyway, but he finally got better.

speaker: HA
words: But then when he got to be about twelve, oh maybe nine, ten I don't know, he

speaker: HA
words: was riding his coaster wagon down the lane, down toward the bottom of the hill.

speaker: HA
words: It was the 4th of July, and they had some friends over, but he caught his leg

speaker: HA
words: in the fence and took out a big piece on his knee or under his knee and so dad

speaker: HA
words: sewed that thing back. And no anesthetics. I've never saw anything like it.

speaker: HA
words: But that wasn't the worst of it. He finally got over that, and he had that scar.

speaker: HA
words: But next thing, when ha was oh fourteen, fifteen, fourteen maybe, and he got

speaker: HA
words: ringworm, he used to like cats, you know play with cats. Anyway, he got ringworm

speaker: HA
words: in his hair and all over his body, big as dollars. And so he thought he should

speaker: HA
words: go to a doctor, but we always cured it with turpentine and lard. And that would

speaker: HA
words: kill it, turpentine would kill about anything you know, but lard kept it from

speaker: HA
words: burning.

speaker: HA
words: But he went to the doctor in Troy, and he put this black iodine, painted all

speaker: HA
words: those sores with black iodine. And he come home and it was burning him up, you

speaker: HA
words: know. One burn is bad enough, but to have twelve, fifteen burns all over your

speaker: HA
words: body that he thought...Dad called him up, "What you do? Boy you're going to kill

speaker: HA
words: him," he said. And he said, "Put some vasoline on it." So he put vasoline on

speaker: HA
words: all those sores, even lard would have done it too. They put bandages on it, and

speaker: HA
words: the next morning the flesh just dropped out in hunks, and it made holes, just

speaker: HA
words: holes in his skin, everywhere. And then of course that flesh came out, and it

speaker: HA
words: finally healed up. But they used vasoline, I don't know if they had anything

speaker: HA
words: else that they could use in those days to heal 'em up again. But that poor

speaker: HA
words: boy sure suffered. It's just terrible. It's just too bad, we shouldn't've let

speaker: HA
words: him go and let Anderson do a thing like that. Iodine in those days was just

speaker: HA
words: black old paste you know...oh it was terrible.

speaker: LS
words: It was really strong stuff?

speaker: HA
words: Oh, the flesh dropped out about a whole inch or better deep wherever it was.

speaker: HA
words: He had scars all his life from that. But, see he'd gone through so much, bad

speaker: HA
words: things I think. But you know he went on to high school and through the University.

speaker: HA
words: And he took electronics, or electrical engineering, electronics I think it was.

speaker: HA
words: And he got a hundred mind you on his test. Then he was going to go back to Chicago

speaker: HA
words: to a school, and then the first test they gave him, then he got another hundred

speaker: HA
words: test right there, the first test he took. Anyway, they said they'd give him a

speaker: HA
words: job. Well they give him a job selling washing machines and crazy stuff like

speaker: HA
words: that, you know. They promised 'em some kind of job. And my brother was going

speaker: HA
words: to school in Rock Island, that's not too fer from Chicago. Anyway there was a

speaker: HA
words: gang I think, I'm not sure whether he had a car or something else, but anyway they

speaker: HA
words: had gangsters that would steal cars and take 'em to a certain garage, and you'd

speaker: HA
words: have to pay so much to get 'em back and so forth, you know. It was just a gang,

speaker: HA
words: a couple of gangsters there that was stealing and selling and exchanging, something

speaker: HA
words: like that. But anyway Ted finally came back home... (Break)

speaker: LS
words: ...About the tuberculosis, and you can leave out any names you know of people...

speaker: HA
words: Yeah.

speaker: LS
words: ...tell about it on the ridge here...

speaker: HA
words: What I was going to say about. We were many many people on Burnt Ridge going

speaker: HA
words: to school you know, the highest was around, oh I think there was 46 some say it

speaker: HA
words: could have been 50 earlier, more than that. We were an awful bunch of kids going

speaker: HA
words: to this one room schoolhouse.

speaker: LS
words: Let me ask you. How did they treat the kids who had tuberculosis?

speaker: HA
words: Well, there they always said—well they didn't know so much about tuberculosis

speaker: HA
words: in those days. People were harder up and didn't have the food, I think they

speaker: HA
words: probably were, many of them probably, well you wouldn't say...The people were good

speaker: HA
words: cooks, and they had gardens,but they always thought then that they should have

speaker: HA
words: fresh air, and they always thought about milk being a good product. But I...I

speaker: HA
words: think too, but I would dare to mention that...I don't think the people, they

speaker: HA
words: didn't have very many clothes. Most people maybe wore one suit of underwear all

speaker: HA
words: week you know, and the next week you'd put on another suit of underwear, that's

speaker: HA
words: in the wintertime you know. And lots of times you'd make dresses out of maybe

speaker: HA
words: men's wool pants, you know the pant legs, you'd make petticoats. Of course

speaker: HA
words: everybody wore 'em because they had to walk to school, they'd walk those three

speaker: HA
words: miles to school. And so our clothes probably wasn't really warm enough for lots

speaker: HA
words: of people and then they had so far to go and probably didn't eat a big enough

speaker: HA
words: breakfast for one thing, too.

speaker: HA
words: But I think you know, it wasn't enough sanitation I think more than anything

speaker: HA
words: else. But I do think that most all this was cause from getting tuberculosis

speaker: HA
words: was through the water bucket. Because nobody else had it afterwards. And no

speaker: HA
words: body else got it after we got the drinking fountain. So I think maybe we'd

speaker: HA
words: better just lay it to that. "That many of the school children got T. B. from

speaker: HA
words: drinking water and would you say...drinking water with a pail with a dipper in

speaker: HA
words: it. Water bucket we had a water bucket." (Speaking while writing this down.)

speaker: LS
words: Once someone got tuberculosis were you not supposed to go near them?

speaker: HA
words: The craziest thing, you never knew if they had tuberculosis. Nobody know

speaker: HA
words: they had tuberculosis I think till they got sick enough and weak and puny that

speaker: HA
words: they stayed home. I think that's the only way it went, see. It went so far that

speaker: HA
words: nobody knew, like the Pearsons up here where they got all that tin on the barns

speaker: HA
words: and the house. They had two boys that died, and they hemorrhaged every so often

speaker: HA
words: and vomittedip blood. And I think maybe Clara she was a nurse, but she died too.

speaker: HA
words: But she died from blood poison, but I think her blood was so thin that you know

speaker: HA
words: blood poison. But she never looked real well she was so pale and so forth, but

speaker: HA
words: I wouldn't dare to mention her, but she died by blood poison by getting a scratch

speaker: HA
words: or something just in two or three days. But these two boys they were sick, and

speaker: HA
words: they put 'em in tents. The Pearson's were in tents outside and stuff. And my

speaker: HA
words: sister, my oldest sister she was sent to Denver, Colorado to the sanitarium there.

speaker: HA
words: But my youngest sister, she was fifteen and dad put a tent out here, she slept

speaker: HA
words: out there in the tent. But...

speaker: LS
words: Is that so that they'd get lots of fresh air?

speaker: HA
words: Fresh air, and maybe too they wouldn't be around the rest of the children too.

speaker: HA
words: But I still think...today you know you can cure T. B. in six months easy, maybe

speaker: HA
words: sooner than that with penicillin or sulphur or something. But those days. But

speaker: HA
words: I think they needed more nourishing food than they possibly get you know. You

speaker: HA
words: probably had too much of the same thing. You know how it is in those days. But

speaker: HA
words: we were much more fortunate, but I think my oldest sister worked too hard.

speaker: HA
words: Because she used to carry water from down at the bottom of the hill and up the

speaker: HA
words: lane to the house, and you know with a big family that water bucket was always

speaker: HA
words: empty. And that was no good for her, and I think she worked too hard. She

speaker: HA
words: looked like...

speaker: LS
words: You got water from down by the creek?

speaker: HA
words: Well, it was a spring, well down there, and anyway they carried it up, and

speaker: HA
words: when they washed they took a little strag or sled they called it. Sled—just

speaker: HA
words: boards across two runners, and they may have had metal on the bottom of those

speaker: HA
words: runners, I don't know, but it just two boards turned up on the ends like a sled

speaker: HA
words: so it would haul a barrel full of water whenever they washed clothes. Well, we

speaker: HA
words: didn't really do that very long here, but anyway she was the oldest girl and she

speaker: HA
words: probably had a lot of the brunt, you know hard work, and here a new house, and

speaker: HA
words: the house not finished you know. Dad built the house so much and then added to.

speaker: HA
words: But just when they first come here maybe she worked to hard. And Auntie Oiler

speaker: HA
words: up here, she died with tuberculosis and she was in a tent. And Ernie's mother

speaker: HA
words: died with T. B. now and she was in Troy. See it was just in the community.

speaker: HA
words: And another thing, we had communion cup. Well like they always say that

speaker: HA
words: with wine, real wine you know is supposed to kill germs and stuff like that, so

speaker: HA
words: you shouldn't get it from the next person. But people'd drink out of the one cup,

speaker: HA
words: and you're bound to, I'm sure it wasn't healthy you know. Some people had big

speaker: HA
words: long mustaches and everything else, and beards and everything else. But then

speaker: HA
words: Pastor Byrd come and he thought it was unlawful to have the single cup for

speaker: HA
words: sanitation sake. They always figured that if you have faith enough you wouldn't

speaker: HA
words: get tuberculosis from that, or the wine was a purifier, but I know we didn't like

speaker: HA
words: it for that reason either. So I was glad when they said that they would have

speaker: HA
words: individual glasses, and that's so much better.

speaker: HA
words: Anyway drinking water, a water bucket with the dipper in it. "The school

speaker: HA
words: children got tuberculosis from drinking water from a water bucket with a dipper

speaker: HA
words: that all of us drank out of the same dipper, and carried the germs. After dad

speaker: HA
words: bought a drinking container where everyone had to have their own cup, no one else

speaker: HA
words: got T. B." (Helen Anderson reads what she has written down.) Yeah, nobody else

speaker: HA
words: got T. B.

speaker: LS
words: Could you tell me some about the prune dryer that your father had?

speaker: HA
words: Oh yeah, yeah that was easy. Anyway, dad had a prune dryer. We had about 20

speaker: HA
words: acres of fruit—prunes and prune trees, oh we had pears and apples, and pea...oh

speaker: HA
words: we had peaches too. They grew wonderful until the frost got 'em. We had 'em for

speaker: HA
words: quite a few years. But prunes that was interesting, but that harvest lasted a

speaker: HA
words: long time. And ray two oldest boys (brothers) stayed out of public school, stayed

speaker: HA
words: out of school in the fall, because we had so many prunes to pick. And dad dried

speaker: HA
words: prunes in a big prune dryer. It had a kiln, a kiln is where you put a fire under

speaker: HA
words: the building, it would be like an oven that would dry all these prunes. And we

speaker: HA
words: had around, I couldn't say, close to maybe oh maybe forty-eight doors, big doors,

speaker: HA
words: like a house door. And inside these doors, we had about twenty-four on each side,

speaker: HA
words: and inside would be a lot of grates that you'd put in there. Just like you'd

speaker: HA
words: put in oven grates, but these were wooden grates made out of wood, wooden sticks.

speaker: HA
words: And they were about maybe thirty-six by forty long, maybe longer than that, but

speaker: HA
words: it was that long anyway, thirty-six or better. And each grate was that big. And

speaker: HA
words: in one door you'd put maybe twelve, fifteen grates at least in each door. And

speaker: HA
words: they were all filled with prunes on each grate. And then these twenty-four doors

speaker: HA
words: went clear around on both sides, and the big fire that they call a kiln would dry

speaker: HA
words: these prunes. And then they'd have to change the ones from the bottom and put

speaker: HA
words: 'em to the top, so the top ones would come down to the bottom so they'd dry more

speaker: HA
words: evenly. And dad would haul—I should say that he washed these prunes in three

speaker: HA
words: or four buckets of—well first a bath of lye water...
